data.title: Tapestry: Premium Valuation, Questionable Growth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Tapestry (NYSE:TPR) appears to be pricing in overly optimistic growth expectations in both revenue and profitability, despite ongoing macroeconomic uncertainties and internal challenges. We see fading Gen Z momentum in North America due to market saturation, and we are skeptical that the company can continue to rely on pricing to drive profitability gains. In our view, investors have been overly influenced by recent positive results while overlooking the macro risks ahead. For these reasons, we rate Tapestry a SELL.

Company Overview

Tapestry offers handbags, accessories, footwear, and other lifestyle products targeted to premium customers. The company has three reportable brands and operating segments: Coach, the largest and the oldest, which represents 80% of total revenues; Kate Spade, representing 17% of total revenues; and Stuart Weitzman (now sold), a New York City-based global luxury footwear brand, representing the remaining 3% of total revenues.

Alternatively, we can segment Tapestry’s business by product line. In FY25, excluding Stuart Weitzman, Handbags represent roughly 56% of sales, Accessories (such as small leather goods, wristlets, pouches, and cosmetic cases) represent 27% of revenues, Footwear takes 6%, while other products collectively represent 11% of revenues. Tapestry reaches its target customers through direct-to-consumer (87%), which includes both digital and brick-and-mortar stores, wholesale (12%), and licensing businesses (1%).

Profitability

At an aggregate level, profitability is improving. However, the only segment driving this positive performance is Coach, which grew 10% in FY25 and keeps its gross margin high at 78%.

Due to negative business trends in the Kate Spade brand (-15% revenue growth in the last 2 years, -11% gross profit in the same period) and the impact of US tariffs, the company recorded an asset write-down of $854.8 million in FY25, driving GAAP Net Income margin to 2.6%.

The other minor segment, Stuart Weitzman, recorded an operating loss in the last two years. After lengthy and unsuccessful turnaround efforts, the company decided to sell the brand in a transaction completed on August 4th.

Digital is the main revenue driver

Given that most of Tapestry’s revenue comes from direct-to-consumer sales (which includes both brick-and-mortar and digital), one might incorrectly use revenue per square footage as a driver.

This would show that for Coach, both stores in North America (61.2% of revenues) and in the rest of the world (38.8%) are performing well, with y-o-y growth in revenue per square footage of 11.3% in American stores and 5.3% internationally.

For Kate Spade, while international stores show resilience, North America shows an 11.6% decline in revenue per square footage. Given that 78% of Kate Spade's revenues are generated in the US, this decline hits heavy.

However, brick-and-mortar stores are growing at only a low single-digit rate, while overall revenues grew by 6%, meaning the revenue growth can instead be largely attributed to the digital channel, which grew at a mid-teens rate in FY25. This growth can be attributed to a (quite successful) social media campaign for Coach targeting Gen Z customers, specifically in North America, where Tapestry’s penetration in the Gen Z market is 6% in aggregate and 4% for the Coach brand alone.

Gen Z Marketing Campaign is Stalling

As shown in the chart depicting new customers for Coach, the marketing efforts undoubtedly paid out. However, what we would like to point your attention to is the rate of growth in the last two quarters, with the line for Total New Consumer growth being lower than the peak in Q2, and with Gen Z growth basically stalling. We view this information, together with the already high penetration for Gen Z customers in the USA (Coach’s largest market), as a sign of market saturation.

A 4% penetration rate might seem low to justify market saturation. However, the way Tapestry defines Gen Z Total Addressable Market is opaque, claiming that there are 19 million 18-to-27-year-old "qualified" females in North America (USA and Canada). According to the latest census data, there are roughly 21.9 million females aged between 18 and 27 in the United States, and about 2 million in Canada. This brings us to a total of 23.9 million females in North America between the ages of 18 and 27, implying that their “qualifying” criterion includes 79.5% of the population.

Given the premium/luxury target price, we deem it highly unlikely the real Total Addressable Market is 19 million people, as we do not buy the thesis of mid-to-low-income consumers potentially spending close to $1000 for a leather handbag. Hence, we believe the % penetration to be severely understated, providing strength to our thesis of market saturation.

Google Trends Point to Slowing Interest in Coach

Searching for "Coach" on Google Trends shows that the overall interest in the brand has increased by almost 20% since last year. This is consistent with the increase in Gen Z customers shown in the previous chart from Q4 24 to Q4 25, but we can also see a decline in interest in the last months.

To exclude any seasonality in our research and evaluate Coach’s recent performance, we have compared Coach’s traffic with other brands such as Michael Kors, Tory Burch, Longchamp, A.P.C., and Prada.

As can be seen from the table showing the % change in online traffic, Coach indeed outperformed its peers in the last year. However, looking at shorter timeframes, we can verify that Coach achieved what we would call an average performance in its peer group, while Tapestry’s stock price continues to price in exceptional growth.

We believe this finding strengthens our thesis of diminishing marginal returns for the Gen Z marketing campaigns.

Profitability Benefits from Price Increases; Tariffs Pose Threat

Tapestry increased prices ahead of tariffs, which are expected to have an impact of $160 million in FY26, assuming a 30% mitigation. This would translate to a negative 230 basis points impact on gross margin, given that manufacturers of Coach products are primarily located in Vietnam, Cambodia, the Philippines, and India, while Kate Spade products are manufactured in Vietnam, Cambodia, China, and the Philippines. This increase in price translated entirely into gross margin expansion, as thanks to Tapestry’s acceleration in inventory purchases, there has been no tariff impact in FY25 (ended in June).

Gross margin for Q4 FY25 was at 76.3%, an increase of 130 basis points compared to Q4 FY24 gross margin of 74.9%. Pricing increases positively contributed around 95 basis points to gross margin expansion, with lower cost of goods sold contributing 45 basis points.

The increase in price benefited profitability, allowing gross margin to reach its highest level in 17 years during Q4 FY25, becoming higher than many luxury peers like LVMH, Kering, Hermès, and close to Prada’s 80% gross margin. Gross margins can be seen as a proxy for brand strength, as consumers are willing to overpay by more than four times and, in exchange, receive a product that is relatively cheaper to make.

Given the industry-high gross margin, we are skeptical that Tapestry will be able to continue using pricing as a lever to increase gross margins, as we believe further pricing increases will shift consumers to other alternatives.

Recent Price Action & the Current Tariff Environment

The company’s stock initially behaved as one would expect when US tariffs on imports were announced, declining 33% from February to April. Then, Tapestry made an impressive (and excessive) recovery, growing 75% since April’s lows, fueled by positive news and excitement thanks to different trade deals. As of today, tariffs on countries in which Tapestry manufactures its products are as follows:

  • Vietnam: 20% tariff deal agreed in July and effective August 7th
  • Cambodia: 19% tariff imposed during Liberation Day on August 1st
  • Philippines: 19% tariff effective July 22nd
  • China: 30% announced on June 11th
  • India: 50% effective August 27th

Especially for India and China, we believe there is a risk of tariff escalation due to their involvement in BRICS and trade relationships with Russia. In recent days, the Trump administration requested the European Union to impose 100% tariffs on India and China as a punishment for buying Russian oil, assuring the US would follow closely.

Given our negative view of Europe’s swift decisiveness, we deem it unlikely that a proper escalation will follow, but these ongoing tensions could have an impact on investors’ sentiment due to around 15% of manufacturing happening in either India or China.

A Track Record of Overpromising and Underdelivering

Following its Q4 and full-year results presentation, Tapestry’s stock fell 15.7% to $95.70 after reporting weaker topline and bottom-line figures. Guidance for revenue growth was limited to the low single digits, partly because Stuart Weitzman is no longer consolidated, while profitability declined due to higher-than-expected tariffs and forex headwinds.

Excluding the sale of Stuart Weitzman, management’s implied revenue growth would have been about 6.3%. This suggests that Coach continues to grow at close to 10% annually, largely driven by digital, while Kate Spade’s turnaround efforts remain focused on slowing revenue declines.

We remain skeptical of management’s assurances, given the failed turnaround attempt at Stuart Weitzman and the overly optimistic guidance for Kate Spade presented during Investor Day 2022 in New York.

Valuation

Tapestry is far from cheap. The company trades at an EV/revenue multiple of 3.5x, about 75% above its three-year average, while profitability multiples are even more stretched: EV/EBIT at 17x and P/E at 19.6x against a three-year average of 10.5x and 11.1x, respectively.

Relative valuation also looks demanding. Tapestry is not undervalued when compared to peers such as Ralph Lauren (EV/Revenue of 2.57x) and is priced on par with the more diversified global luxury leader LVMH (EV/Revenue of 3.37x and EV/EBIT of 15.7x).

Based on our multiple valuation, we believe a more normal range for Tapestry would be around 15x next-twelve-months P/E and a 2.6x NTM EV/Revenues multiple, which combined imply a price at around $80 per share.

Investment Risks

Our investment thesis fundamentally relies on disappointing revenue growth in the upcoming quarters due to weaknesses in Gen Z marketing campaigns. If, instead, Tapestry manages to spur a new wave of interest in Coach, and revenue continues to grow at or above the market's expectations, the stock price could continue to increase.

We are also skeptical about the turnaround potential in Kate Spade. However, it is worth noting that, in August 2024, Tapestry replaced the CEO & Brand President of Kate Spade. The brand president didn't manage to soften the decline of the brand during the last fiscal year, but one year might not be enough time to evaluate the effectiveness of the new CEO.

Additionally, Tapestry's stock had an impressive recovery from its $92.62 low reached after the Q4 earnings release. If this positive momentum continues, we believe it could last until November, when we expect Tapestry will disappoint during the next quarterly presentation.

Takeaway

The company appears to be pricing in a smooth path to growth thanks to Coach's digital strength. We instead believe that, due to market saturation in the US, the Gen Z-focused digital marketing campaign is stalling.

Geopolitical risks, primarily escalating tensions between China and India due to Russian oil, pose additional macroeconomic headwinds. We don't think a successful turnaround in Kate Spade is likely, given the previous failed efforts for Stuart Weitzman. Finally, we believe additional price hikes could drive customers away from Tapestry and to other premium competitors.

For these reasons, we rate Tapestry a SELL.

data.title: AutoZone: High ROIC, Steady Demand, Modest Buy On EPS Compression
data.description:
Intro

The average age of a vehicle in America - 12.8 years - is among the longest in the world. Older cars need more replacement parts, providing a powerful structural tailwind for AutoZone (NYSE:AZO), the largest aftermarket car parts retailer in the US. AutoZone's other two major markets - Brazil and Mexico - also retain their cars for years. This creates that demand that helps AutoZone remain a growth and return generator.

However, the company's latest Q4 numbers, just out this week, seem to tell a different story. Net sales of $6.2 billion were up a mere 0.6% year over year, and EPS fell 5.6% to $48.71. Looking closely, though, the picture is much more robust: adjusting for the additional week last year, sales were actually up 6.9%, domestic same-store sales rose 4.8%, and international constant-currency comps gained 7.2%.

Thus, demand was fine, after all, but the real pressure came from elsewhere: one, a non-cash LIFO charge that dragged gross margin down by 128 basis points, and two, expansion and supply store-related ramp-up costs. In this article, I argue that both are temporary headwinds, not structural difficulties, and once they are gone, the stock will pick up pace.

My thesis is pretty simple: AutoZone has been a strong buyback company with high ROIC, and if the market overreacts to the GAAP margin or EPS drag, that's a buying opportunity right there. I believe LIFO will stabilize with cost inflation, and ramp-up costs start generating value as stores and DCs mature. Underlying growth drivers like aging cars, stable demand, and disciplined capital management will then start delivering value.

Flashback to Prior Performance

Before I dive into Q4 numbers to support my thesis, I think it is worth studying AutoZone's past performance. Looking at data from 2020 to 2024, I note that net sales grew at a CAGR of 10%, from $12.6bn in 2020 to $18.5bn in 2024. There were several years of strong growth after the pandemic, followed by a soft FY24, which saw US same-store sales grow only 0.4%. Operating profit grew at 11% CAGR, from $2.4 billion to $3.8 billion. This highlights not only scale but also margin improvement, which, at 19-21%, was far above most retailers.

AutoZone provides world-class Return on invested capital (ROIC), which ranged from 35.7% in FY20 to 55.4% in FY23, normalizing to 49.7% in FY24. AutoZone also has one of the most comprehensive share buyback programs in corporate America. Since 1998, it has retired over 80% of its shares, which, together with operating profit growth, is powering EPS growth, which has more than doubled in five years. To top it all, operating cash flow has stayed consistently around the ~$3bn zone annually, easily funding share repurchases, new store growth, and supply chain investments.

These splendid numbers took a beating in 2024, when both the US DIY and DIFM businesses faced growth pressure for the first time in years. There was a pullback in discretionary parts purchases, accounting for ~15% of business, along with inflation. However, same-store sales growth in the international segment was robust.

Q4 and FY25 Results vs. Prior Backdrop

Against that strong but slightly slowing backdrop, Q4 and FY 2025 results look mixed: demand is intact, but reported profitability is under pressure.

Top-line strength

As I noted in the beginning, adjusting for last year's extra week, reported sales grew by a healthy 6.9%, showing the core business demand remains intact. Unlike FY24's poor showing of 0.4% growth, domestic comps grew 4.8% this time, relieving shareholders. Both DIY and DIFM segments improved sequentially QoQ, showing momentum going into FY26. On a constant currency basis, international comps also grew at a robust 7.2%. All of this goes to show that demand for business is solid, and growing.

Margin compression

With margins, however, the picture didn't look so good on the surface. Gross margin fell 98 bps to 51.5%. Looking closely, the decline was almost entirely due to a 128 bps non-cash LIFO charge ($80M). Excluding LIFO, underlying merchandise margins actually improved slightly.

Operating expenses went up from 31.6% to 32.4% of sales. This was due to the company's heavy spending on long term growth initiatives, including at least one new US DC and 141 stores in this quarter alone; in FY25, they added 304, which is higher than the 213 stores added in 2024.

While these spends support long-term growth, they drove operating profit down 7.8% to ~$1.2B and EPS down 5.6% to $48.71.

Full-year context

Taking the entire year, FY25 net sales again showed a 2.4% growth, to $18.9bn, but EPS fell 3.1% to $144.87. Once again, gross margin slipped 55 bps, which was almost entirely due to a $64mn LIFO charge vs. a $40mn benefit last year.

AutoZone also spent less on share repurchase this year, $1.5bn versus $3.2bn last year. While this was still meaningful at $3,425/share, and share count reduction supported EPS, GAAP earnings lagged, ultimately pulling the buyback volume down.

Here's a comprehensive picture of what's good in Q4 vs. what's not so good:

Why LIFO and Ramp-Up Costs Are Temporary Headwinds

This weakness in AutoZone's Q4 earnings was not demand-driven. Demand was just fine - it was only the effect of two temporary forces, non-cash LIFO accounting charges and ramp-up costs tied to growth investments - that brought margin/EPS down.

LIFO-adjustments - non-cash and cyclical: There are two accounting methods for inventory - LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) and FIFO (First-In, First-Out). AutoZone uses LIFO, so, even if they bought a product for, say, $80 last year, and another for $100 this year, and then sold the first product for $120 now, they assume a margin of $20 ($120-$100) and not $40 ($120-$80). Here, rising input costs ($80->$100) raised the cost of goods sold, depressing paper margins. In Q4, AutoZone shaved 128 bps off margins with a $80mn charge, so even though merchandise margins actually improved, reported earnings showed a decline. Similarly, for FY2025, cumulative LIFO charges were $64mn, compared to a $40mn benefit last year.

What we need to understand is that LIFO is non-cash - it has no effect on cash flow. We just need to wait for cost inflation to stabilize. At that time, LIFO adjustments will make margin optics look normal again. That means AutoZone's true merchandise margin performance (which was positive this quarter) is stronger than GAAP suggests.

Ramp-Costs - Investments That Will Leverage: There was a high increase in operating expenses due to investments in new distribution centers, higher store growth, and international expansion. Although these investments will bear fruit once these stores mature, they are creating pressure on current gross margins. It is to be noted, though, that AutoZone has an ROIC of above 50%, and its previous expansion cycles have helped produce these very high returns. I have no doubt that this cycle will not be any different.

Evidence From Merchandise Margins: Despite gross margins dropping, merchandise margins actually improved, according to the company. While they offer no actual figures, this is still the best proof that core business is as healthy as before. LIFO and ramp-up costs are simply accounting and timing issues, and should not be treated as structural faults. If the market overreacts to this headline (stock was down around 3% on 22nd Sep), that should be considered a buy-the-dip opportunity.

Peer Comparison: AutoZone vs. O'Reilly vs. Advance

Two key competitors I will discuss are O'Reilly Automotive (ORLY) and Advance Auto Parts (AAP). Here's the necessary data in a table:

In gross margin, AZO has a slight edge over ORLY and a strong edge over AAP, implying better cost control and product mix. Both AZO and ORLY have profitability in a different league from AAP. Similarly for net margin, which shows the first two companies' superiority in converting sales to profits. In terms of ROIC, too, AZO and ORLY are on a different level altogether, although here too, ORLY has a slight edge over AZO.

Intrinsic Valuation

Here, I take the following base figures from FY25:

Revenue - $18.9bn, operating margin - 19%, EBIT - $3.6bn, net income - $2.5bn, diluted EPS - $144.87. All these figures are available here.

My key assumptions are - revenue growth will be 7% CAGR for the next 5 years. Adjusted sales growth was 6.9% in Q4, so this is not implausible. Operating margin, I assume, will normalize to 21% once the LIFO effect fades; I use FY25 as base year, ignoring LIFO effect this year. This implies normalized FY25 net income of ~$3.0B, versus reported GAAP $2.5B. Tax rate I assume to be long term effectively 24%. I could reduce buyback a little, to $1.5bn to $2bn, from 2024 figures, given the reduction this year. This is 2-3% share count reduction per year - lower, but still material - I will peg it at 3.5% a little optimistically. I use a WACC of 8%. This is slightly on the higher side from Professor Damodaran's figures. Terminal growth I take to be 3%.

My modest bull case gives me an intrinsic value per share of around $4,610. This is still around 20% above current price levels, which is why I said this is a modest small pilot and buy-the-dip opportunity.

Risks

We have seen a DIY weakness in FY24, which has been somewhat mitigated in Q4, but we need to see a sustained rebound in this very important metric. US DIY is AZO's largest revenue driver, so any weakness here can have a strong impact.

We have also seen a margin pressure in Q4, and while the LIFO effect should subside quickly, the ramp-up effects may take longer until the new stores/DCs mature. That could still continue pressuring margin.

One positive we have noted is the strong share buyback. However, its flipside is that EPS growth is heavily dependent on repurchases, so if cash flow dips in some quarter, there could be a pullback in EPS compounding. One way this could happen is increasing competition from online retail like Amazon and other players, who generally have strong margins. We have seen in FY25 that there's a significant reduction in buyback volume. If that downtrend continues, EPS will be pressured.

Summary & Investment Thesis

Here, I have argued that the LIFO and ramp-up-related EPS pressure is temporary. Merchandise margins are rising, comps have recovered from last year's weakness, and stores are being opened quickly, demonstrating confidence. AutoZone still generates world-class ROIC (~50%), runs ~20% operating margins, and maintains one of the most aggressive buyback programs in corporate history.

From my valuation analysis, the stock is almost fairly valued today, with modest upsides if the bull case I outlined holds steady. However, the market often punishes GAAP EPS declines, even if they are actually effects of temporary swings. If that happens, this becomes a decent buying opportunity. As it seems almost fairly valued right now, I would suggest a small pilot and then a buy-the-dip approach if the stock drops below $3900.

data.title: KB Home: Ongoing Softness As The Backlog Shrinks
data.description:
Shares of KB Home (NYSE:KBH) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing 28%. Mortgage rates have stayed stubbornly high despite Fed rate cuts, hampering demand and pricing power given a challenged affordability backdrop. With KBH's focus on first-time homebuyers, it is more exposed to affordability strains than peers, an added headwind for results. That said, shares have recovered from their lows, rising 22% since I last covered them in June when I rated KBH a "hold." Given its performance, a buy rating could have been justified, though its performance is just in line with other builders I have been more bullish on, like Toll (TOL). With updated financials and an ever-changing macro outlook, now is a good time to revisit KBH.

Demand Was Soft in Q3 Given First-Time Buyer Exposure

In the company's fiscal third quarter, KB Home reported earnings of $1.61, which beat estimates by $0.10 even as revenue declined by 7% to $1.62 billion. This beat was largely driven by costs, as KBH has been able to shorten build times, as noted on the earnings call, reducing costs and offsetting price pressure. However, underlying demand trends remain weak, given challenged affordability in this elevated rate environment.

As you can see below, about half of its buyers are first-time buyers. This cohort generally has to "stretch" the most to make a purchase. This can make demand even more volatile in reaction to rate changes, especially in the current environment where affordability is particularly difficult. Because it caters to entry-level buyers, I view KBH as one of the riskier homebuilders with heightened sensitivity to fluctuations in the marketplace.

During the quarter, deliveries were down 7% to 3,393, and prices continue to soften, with KBH reporting a $476k selling price. KBH reported an 18.9% gross margin, down 180 bps from a year ago, given softening pricing. Additionally, while tariffs are a relatively minor cost for a builder, they still lead to some added costs, and given the softness of the housing market, I do not believe builders are well-equipped to pass on these costs to consumers, further straining margins. Given a lower gross margin and lost operating leverage, KB had an 8.8% operating margin, down 210 bps from last year.

KBH's Backlog is Shrinking

Now, deliveries have been supported by KBH's backlog, but that buffer continues to shrink. It now has 4,333 homes in its backlog. That leaves it with a $1.99 billion backlog, down over $900 million from a year earlier. KBH, like most builders, has been delivering more homes than it has received orders for, gradually shrinking its backlog. This is why a backlog exists-to smooth revenue during downturns. However, with its backlog down to less than 4 months of revenue, it provides much less cushion than it once did, and we are likely to see KBH need to slow deliveries to the level of orders within the next 12 months. I would also note that there was a 17% cancellation rate, a tick up from 15% a year ago, which further challenges the backlog.

In Q3, KBH once again had fewer orders than deliveries. It had 2,950 net orders, down 4% from a year earlier, and it is a mild positive to see that its orders are now falling more slowly than its deliveries. As you can see below, orders lagged deliveries by about 400 units. However, one modest piece of good news was that orders have begun to improve in the Southeast. Florida pricing has been especially weak, given excess supply, and this lower pricing may now be helping to support demand a bit.

Geographic Exposure is an Ongoing Challenge

One overarching challenge for KB Home, and many of the homebuilders, is that while home prices are flat in the nation over the past year, there has been significant geographic dispersion. The South and the West have generally seen falling home prices, whereas the Midwest and Northeast have been stronger. This divergence largely reflects markets that have seen more supply vs. less supply. Essentially, the markets where builders like KBH have the largest presence are performing the worst, as there is a struggle absorbing this supply. As such, I expect KBH's pricing to lag the national trend at least into mid-2026.

Alongside results, KBH cut sales guidance from $6.3-$6.5 billion to $6.1-$6.2 billion, while home prices will be in the lower half of its prior $480-$490k range. This guidance implies a Q4 selling price of about $470k. It narrowed its gross margin forecast to the midpoint of its prior range at 19.2%-19.3%. This essentially implies KBH sees Q4 showing an ongoing deceleration in housing-related activity, though given cost cuts, incremental margin pressure will be modest.

Mortgage Rates May Not Fall Further, but Fading Supply is Helpful

The question is whether we can see a rebound in demand in 2026. Now in September, we have seen a notable drop in mortgage rates as increased labor market worries have reduced long-term yields. I would emphasize that mortgage rates are more closely tied to the 10-year treasury than the Fed funds rate. This decline in rates has raised hopes that we can see a rebound in homebuying activity, though I believe a drop below 6% is a more critical level. Even with Fed rate cuts, I am cautious about forecasting a further decline in mortgage rates given persistent US debt issuance and inflation worries. Indeed, rates have been ticking up back from their low last week.

While I am cautious about seeing that much of a rate-driven demand surge, one positive is that builders are building less. As you can see below, the number of homes under construction continues to fall. In particular, builders are curtailing speculative construction. Indeed, KBH wants to return to build-to-order to 70% from 50% currently. I was encouraged that its lot portfolio is down 6% from last year as KBH pulls back in this soft environment. Decreasing supply is a reason why I do not see prices falling more than 3-5%. That said, I struggle to see a meaningful turn higher in pricing or volume until rates sustainably crack 6%.

KBH has a solid balance sheet with $331 million of cash and $1.94 billion of debt, giving it a 33.2% debt-to-capital. Given the weakness in the stock, KBH has been more aggressive in buying back shares, spending $439 million so far this year. As a result, there has been nearly a 12% reduction in its share count. I expect the pace of buybacks to slow a bit, as KBH will want to keep debt to capital in the low to mid 30% range. KBH also pays a 1.6% dividend yield.

At 10x 2026 Earnings, Shares Are a Hold

Shares are trading just above its $60.25 book value. Over the next year, I expect deliveries to continue to fall given KBH's smaller backlog and focus on reducing its speculative building, and I see deliveries declining 3-7% in 2026. In a modestly lower home price environment, it should be able to stabilize margins, given improved productivity, and a lower share count will help to offset some of the pressures. I view KBH as having $6.00-$6.50 in forward earnings power, leaving shares right around 10x earnings.

Given KBH's exposure to the most volatile segment of the housing market, first-time buyers, I struggle to see much multiple expansion, absent a further decline in long-term rates. The business faces persistent softness, and its backlog provides less and less support. As such, I do not see shares as a buy, but book value can provide support, and 10x is not "expensive." As such, I see shares as a hold. Unless you expect a larger drop in rates, I prefer names like Toll, given their exposure to higher-income buyers.

data.title: SuperX AI Technology Pivoting To AI-Training Servers From Interior Design
data.description:
SuperX AI Technology Limited (NASDAQ:SUPX), previously known as Junee Ltd which was an interior-design focused company operating in Hong Kong, has in short order with some acquisitions of mostly empty companies started a new business to try to sell pre-configured with multi-modal (model?) LLM model server boxes. While they've already signed leases for a supply centre in Japan and have apparently launched their flagship server box, we just don't see that much value-add in a pre-configured server box, leveraging other established companies' technology. This integration work for server boxes is also a totally new business for this company and they have no track record in this space. Last year this was an interior design company. While on one hand it's positive that the interior design work can finance the business option of this AI-related business, we feel that this sort of stuff in the markets is emblematic of the later and bubbly stages of a market cycle. We should also mention that due to infrequent reporting of SUPX financials as an emerging growth company, we have not seen any financials since they have started apparently leaning meaningfully into this pivot.

Readers should be aware that there is an investigation into potentially launching a class-action against SUPX right now following a report by J Capital Research.

With likely newfound development costs also constituting a cash burn, we are uncertain about the health of the business, particularly as the interior design business was also under macro pressure in Hong Kong before these new initiatives. We have to see more updated results since after the pivot has started to come into action. There are massive execution risks, no track record and it trades at something like 1000x P/2024 Sales from interior design work. We are not very interested in the interior design business either due to high operating leverage, customer concentration risks and macro risks. It's very uncertain, not helped by the lower reporting requirements for these emerging growth companies. And we would never go forward without more certainty than we have at this moment.

What we've gathered

If one goes back to their latest 20-F, there of course is no mention of SUPX or of AI or anything of the sort. Prior to the name change and strategic pivot starting it would seem in late 2024 and in 2025, the company was an interior design company dealing with the challenges of a recession in the interior design market in Hong Kong. The business has issues such as customer concentration and cost overrun risks associated to inflation. Operating leverage is also considerable.

For the fiscal year ended June 30, 2024, two customers accounted for approximately 34.1% and 21.5% of the Company’s total revenue.

20-F YE June 2024

Then they made a nominal acquisition of a company called ASPAC for a AUD$100, a holding company with no operations. They then entered into an agreement with a company called PanaAI, which they will pay $100 million in instalments as per a customer/service purchase agreement. Here it seems they want to get into the business of building a supercomputing centre in Australia and then selling that computing capacity and PanaAI will supply GPUs among other things for that.

Then Junee/SUPX later acquired a company called MindEnergy, a pre-revenue company with nominal activities. A loan receivable to someone who is now a related party, the company's only significant asset, was the vast majority of the company's balance sheet before it was acquired by SUPX. The total consideration is conditional depending on the repayment of that loan to MindEnergy from Ma Chao, as much as equal to the loan receivable to MindEnergy of around $19 million, which is the only reasonable price for MindEnergy given that they weren't really doing much before getting acquired.

Following these acquisitions they started to move towards providing server boxes that can be used to train AI. Since the close of the last financials, they've launched an integrated out-of-the-box model on a server. When announcements of their first AI product came out, that's when the SUPX stock price really started to rise - at least it didn't rise just on the name change. This was around the end of July. Just before this they signed the lease for the supply hub in Japan - on which we have few details by the way. The server box comes with a pre-trained version of an OpenAI LLM. In order to deploy all this they did an equity financing by selling shares and warrants for an aggregate proceed of $16.2 million. This was as of September 16 2025 that they raised this full amount. We do not know as of now what the run-rate costs are going to be for the company, including development and other costs associated with launching this new product.

This all came out after the close of books ended December 2024. At that point the company was still just a struggling interior design company in Hong Kong, struggling on account of operating leverage and also inflation causing quoted interior design projects to be liquidated less profitably. The growth in operating expenses was on account of legal, compliance and audit fees being incurred, some lease expenses (not related to the Japan facility), SBC and traveling expenses, which for the six months 2024 was close to being as high as operating expenses were overall in for the 6m 2023 ($550k).

Bottom Line

We would want to wait for the next results release to be able to really understand the run-rate business situation. Hardware is usually not that profitable to sell especially when the hard engineering work happens upstream from integration by players like Nvidia (NVDA) and OpenAI (MSFT). The acquisitions they've made are of almost entirely empty companies. They now have a lease in place which they need to leverage to supply the Asian market with these server boxes. They are working with partner PanaAI and will need to pay them very significant money for a service upon which they may not have much to add in order to build a supercomputing centre. To our understanding their server box product announced is not coming from PanaAI and their project with PanaAI is a separate idea, though together it builds towards the vision of being a "one stop shop" for AI-server products and solutions for enterprise. The ~$16 million they've raised is short of their stated target of $40 million associated to the PanaAI build and to working capital needs, likely with working capital required to buy inventory to integrate the server boxes that they now want to sell.

Singapore, October 29, 2024... (the “Company”), today announced its strategic focus on developing and operating artificial intelligence (AI) computing centers. To support this initiative, the Company plans to raise up to US$40 million through a financing round, with the funds earmarked for the establishment of an AI supercomputing center in Australia and to bolster its working capital.

6-K

We have many questions. They are integrating other software and hardware into an enterprise product, and are unsure of the extent of the value-add. Maybe they can add value from the interior design work in carrying out some aspects of building out a datacentre. But we imagine that is highly specialised work and to our knowledge this was not Junee's area of expertise before. We really just have to see what the economics are going to look like and if there's much substance here. We are not interested at all at the moment.

data.title: Playtika: The Market Might Be Underestimating Its Long-Term Growth Strategy
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Ricardo Weder as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

I believe that Playtika Holding (NASDAQ:PLTK) is currently in a successful pivot trajectory, coming from a legacy social casino business to a "high-growth" casual games platform. There is a clear misreading of the market's short-term margin pressure as a sign of decline, creating a great entry point opportunity.

A Story of Two Companies That Rolled into One

Let's talk about numbers, because they will tell the real story. In Q2 2025, Playtika reported a "resilient" quarter with revenues of $696 million.

While this represented a slight sequential decline of 1.4% from Q1, it was an impressive 11.0% YoY increase. These financial results are hiding a deeper story of "strategic transformation." While the company's legacy titles are already showing signs of weakness, a closer look at the data reveals that Playtika is successfully pivoting to a new growth model. The market's focus on this decline is a misreading of a well-executed, data-driven strategy with great value generation potential.

The real thesis is a big one: Playtika is making a massive pivot. They're shifting resources away from their old cash cow games to turbocharge a new fleet of high-growth games. This isn't just business as usual; it is a calculated and high-stakes game of its own, and the latest data suggests they might just be winning.

The Old Guard: Powering the Pivot

For many years, titles like Slotomania and Bingo Blitz were the company's strength and were generating an immense and predictable cash flow. But as every empire has to evolve, in 2024, revenue from Playtika's social casino games fell by approximately $120 million.

Management admitted in their Q2 2025 earnings call that the decline in Slotomania has accelerated, with revenue falling 35.4% year-over-year. The company is actively implementing changes to address the game's economy, a process they describe as "getting worse before it gets better".

The capital generated by the "old guard games" is not sitting idle. It is the war chest funding a high-octane acquisition strategy. The company is using the cash flow from its mature titles to buy its way into the future.

The New Wave: The Engine of Growth

This is where the excitement is. Playtika's secret weapon isn't a single hit game but its proprietary "Playtika Boost Platform." This isn't just corporate jargon; it is the technology that turns acquisitions into instant successes by leveraging AI and machine learning for everything, from user acquisition to monetization.

The proof is in the numbers :

The 2024 acquisition of SuperPlay Ltd. is now fully integrated and drove significant YoY revenue growth. The company's sales and marketing expenses shot up by over $119 million in 2024, according to their annual report, due to this acquisition, a clear sign that they are aggressively investing in these new titles.

The newly launched Disney Solitaire has been a great success. It hit the $100 million annual run-rate revenue threshold already, according to their second quarter 2025 results presentation.

Even veteran casual games are thriving. Bingo Blitz had a record-breaking quarter in Q1 2025 with revenue of $162.4 million, and while it slightly decreased sequentially, it increased 2.9% YoY and set a new record for DTC revenue in Q2.

Financials: Don't Let the Margins Fool You

A look at Playtika's margins will make you nervous. The credit-adjusted EBITDA margin dropped from 32.4% in 2023 to 29.7% in 2024 and was only 24.0% in Q2 2025. Analyzing this, it is not a sign of operational failure; it's a direct consequence of their strategy of capital being deployed.

Management is deliberately sacrificing short-term profitability to increase market share in high-growth areas. They bet that once these new games mature, the marketing expenditures will decrease and margins will increase again. This isn't speculation; it is a well-documented playbook from the likes of Meta and Google. A key point from the Q2 report is the ability to maintain the 2025 Adjusted EBITDA guidance of $715 million to $740 million despite revising revenue guidance downward, which shows strong operational discipline and cost control.

The most important part of this puzzle is that Playtika is focusing on its direct-to-consumer (DTC) platform. They've set a new long-term goal for DTC revenue: 40%, up from 30%, according to their first-quarter results. This is a big deal because in the DTC platform, we are charged 3 to 4% for the payment processing, while third-party app stores like Apple and Google take a huge 30% cut.

Look at Debt and Financial Risks

It is important to review how Playtika manages its substantial debt in the mentioned transition. As of December 31, 2024, Playtika had a significant debt of $2.4 billion in outstanding indebtedness, according to their 10-K.

The company has generated a strong and consistent cash flow, which is its primary source of liquidity. This cash flow has let the company meet its financial obligations and fund acquisitions.

The company generated $119.6 million in free cash flow in the first half of 2025.

The management has been proactive in mitigating debt-related risks, and the company has also successfully extended the maturity of its revolving credit facility to Sep 2027 from March 2026.

This is critical for ensuring it has the financial flexibility to manage liquidity and potential earnout payments from acquisitions like SuperPlay.

This is not a company ignoring its liabilities; they are actively managing them to support its long-term strategic goals.

The Key Metrics

If you want to see the real potential success of this strategy, it is important to look past the headlines and dive into the operational metrics that will generate the future value. They tell a different and more optimistic story.

Daily Paying Users (DPU): This is the ultimate proof of monetization and also future growth. DPU grew dramatically from 298K in Q2 2024 to 378K in Q2 2025, an incredible 26.8% YoY increase. This is a clear signal that the acquisition pipeline is successfully bringing in new paying customers and validating the M&A continuous strategy.

Payer Conversion: The rate at which players convert to paying customers rose from 3.7% in Q2 2024 to 4.3% in Q2 2025. This also shows that Playtika's monetization model is becoming more effective with its new portfolio.

Daily Active Users (DAU): DAUs grew to 8.8 million in Q2 2025, implying an 8.6% increase from the prior year. This indicates that the company is successfully growing its audience despite its focusing on its higher-value players.

Industry Landscape

The mobile gaming industry is highly competitive and fragmented. It is also rapidly evolving and has low barriers to entry. This makes it a "winner take most" market, where a small number of top-grossing games dominate revenue and players' attention. Playtika's advantages and disadvantages must always be viewed through this lens.

The Competitive Advantages

Unrivaled Live Operations: Playtika's core strength is its "best-in-class live game operations" and also the proprietary technology platform. The Playtika Boost Platform uses AI to deliver curated game content and optimize the offers and the time; this is a crucial factor for driving monetization and retaining paying users in the long term. This operational expertise, gained by years of execution, is a significant moat that many competitors lack.

Durable Portfolio and Scale: Unlike many studios that rely on a single fleeting hit, Playtika's strategy focuses on a portfolio of titles with proven longevity. For example, consider that Slotomania was released in 2010 and currently remains as the second-largest game in its portfolio based on revenues as of December 31, 2024. It's clear that scale and durability give the company a powerful financial foundation to fund its growth.

Proven M&A Playbook: Playtika has a highly disciplined approach to acquisitions, with a history of acquiring games and studios at attractive prices and achieving "meaningful synergies." This ability to identify and scale up games is a core competency that differentiates them from competitors.

The Competitive Disadvantages

Intense Competition: The company faces strong competition from major players like Tencent Holdings (OTCPK:TCEHY), Activision Blizzard, Electronic Arts (EA), and Take-Two Interactive Software (TTWO). All of them have greater financial and technical resources. Also, there is always a threat regarding new, innovative players entering the industry.

Platform Dependence: A significant portion of the revenues is generated through third-party platforms like the Apple App Store and Google Play Store, which can change their policies unilaterally. This risk is partially mitigated by Playtika's focus on its DTC platforms, but it remains a potential vulnerability for the future.

Industry innovation and disruption: The industry is subject to rapid changes in technology and player preferences. The emergence of new business models, such as "sweepstakes casino" games, and new entrants in the gaming space, like Netflix, could pose a threat to Playtika's businesses.

The Timing

The market sentiment has been negative, focused on the decline of legacy games and, as mentioned, also the margin pressure. This has suppressed the stock price, creating a disconnect between the company's intrinsic value and its market valuation. This is the classic window of opportunity for a value investor.

Furthermore, Playtika's pivot is now past the initial, high-risk stage. The investments have been made, most notably the $686.9 million acquisition of SuperPlay Ltd., and the early returns are already visible in the key performance indicators like DPU and payer conversion.

The upcoming launch of the new slot game, Jackpot Tour, during the Q4 2025, represents a direct catalyst that could change the narrative around the company's legacy games. Investing now could represent getting in before the market fully appreciates the success of these new growth drivers.

Valuation

Calculating the potential valuation of a company in transition is always complex. It requires looking beyond the raw numbers of today and making a judgment call on how a strategic pivot will affect the business tomorrow.

We begin our valuation by establishing a price target based on a sound, quantitative model. The foundation of our model is the company's own 2025 adjusted EBITDA guidance, projected to be between $715 million and $740 million. The midpoint of this guidance is $727.5 million.

While the market currently prices PLTK at a low forward EV/EBITDA multiple of 4.52x, we apply a conservative and justifiable multiple of 8.0x for our model. This reflects the company's proven ability to generate strong FCF and its successful M&A strategy, which justifies a re-rating closer to its peers.

Multiplying by our midpoint guidance, we arrive at a modeled Enterprise Value (EV) of approximately $5.82 billion, subtracting the company's net debt of approximately $1.8 billion to arrive at an equity value of roughly $4.02 billion. Dividing this by the 376.3 million shares outstanding, our final price target is approximately $10.68 per share.

This suggests a potential growth of approximately 209% from a recent trading price of $3.46. This creates a significant margin of safety.

Risks

There are risks that could make our investment thesis wrong.

Execution Risk: The "New Wave" plan might not work out. If new purchases aren't properly integrated or new games don't get the attention they need, the investment might not pay off and revenue might stay the same.

A lot of competition: The mobile gaming market is very competitive and easy to get into. Zynga and Scopely are always trying to get more users and market share.

Platform Dependency: Playtika needs third-party platforms like the Google Play Store and Apple's App Store to get their games out there and make money. Changing their policies could have a direct impact on how they make money and get new users.

Regulatory Risk: The company's social casino games are what they are, which means there are regulatory risks. Changes to gambling laws in the US and other countries could directly affect how much money its main business makes.

Dividends

Throughout this analysis, we have focused on the operational and financial metrics that validate Playtika's strategic pivot. For those who remain unconvinced, the company dividend is a final point that breaks the conventional narrative. The company has maintained a quarterly dividend of $0.10 per share; this translates to a projected 11.14% forward yield. This generous dividend is a powerful statement of the management's confidence in future profitability. Even with a high payout ratio of 166.67%. It effectively is paying investors to be patient while the company's long-term growth strategy takes hold, turning the market skepticism into a strategic advantage.

Conclusion

The company is clearly undervalued, as the market is fundamentally misinterpreting their strategy. The successful transition from a mature business model to a high-growth one, driven by strategic acquisitions and a superior technology platform, creates an asymmetric investment opportunity.

If the company continues to demonstrate solid growth in its key metrics (DPU, payer conversion) and the market begins to recognize it, Playtika has real potential to grow their stock price exponentially in the next 12 to 24 months.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lithium Miners News For The Month Of September 2025

Welcome to the September 2025 edition of the lithium miner news.

The past month saw lithium prices lower.

A key news item the past month was the U.S. Government looking to take an equity stake in a lithium miner for the first time. This shows the importance of lithium to the US's future national security and industry development.

Lithium price news

Metal.com reported lithium spodumene concentrate Index (Li2O 5.5%-6.2%, excluding tax/insurance/freight) spot price of USD 860, as of September 23, 2025, lower the past month.

The China lithium carbonate spot price was lower last month, now at CNY 73,850. The CIF Asia lithium carbonate price is ~US$9.40/kg.

China lithium carbonate spot price 5 year chart - CNY 73,850 (~USD 10,383) (source)

Lithium demand versus supply outlook

BloombergNEF forecasts lithium deficits may begin in 2025 (as of Oct. 2024) (source)

RMIS forecasts lithium deficits to start after 2028, may grow very large without major new investments (as of May 2025) (source)

Albemarle is forecasting lithium deficits more likely from ~2027/28 (as of mid 2025) (source)

GGII, a Shenzhen-based consultancy, forecasts a balanced lithium market this year, a 10,000t LCE deficit in 2026, and a 60,000t deficit in 2027 (as of mid 2025) (source)

Trend Investing v IEA demand forecast for EV metals (IEA)
