Trend Investing forecasts lithium demand to increase 10.2x this decade.

Note: A Nov. 2020 UBS forecast is for "lithium demand to lift 11-fold from ~400kt in 2021 through to 2030."

Conclusion

China lithium carbonate spot prices and spodumene spot prices were lower the past month.

Highlights for the month were:

  • Global EV battery installations totaled 590.7 GWh from Jan.-July, a 35.3% YoY increase. Market share leaders remain CATL with 37.5% & BYD with 17.8%.
  • CATL's Yichun lithium mine expected to resume production soon, Securities Times reports.
  • CATL hits new high as new China policy powers up Chinese auto, battery stocks. Chinese government to boost its energy-storage sector through 2027, driving about 250 billion yuan (~$35.09B), in project investment.
  • China aims for energy storage installations equivalent to today’s global capacity by 2027.
  • Fastmarkets - All eyes are now on the fate of the other seven Chinese mines being scrutinised. More cutbacks will help tighten the supply-demand balance and prompt the use of inventories, which could underpin prices.
  • Lithium Americas soars on US Government interest.
  • HSBC adjusts Price Target on Albemarle to $75 from $60, maintains hold rating.
  • Ganfeng Lithium: Solid-state batteries trial-fitted & mass-produced in select car models.
  • Tianqi Lithium initiates construction of 50 mt/year lithium sulphide Pilot Project.
  • Chengxin Lithium to acquire 21% stake in Sichuan Qicheng Mining for approximately RMB1.46 billion (USD205 million) in cash.
  • Mineral Resources FY25 results - Revenue $4.5B, down 15% YoY; influenced by weaker iron ore and lithium prices. Underlying NPAT $112M.
  • Liontown Resources share purchase plan raises ~A$56 million.
  • Sayona Mining name change to Elevra Lithium Limited [ASX:ELV] is expected to take effect on 26 September 2025. NAL Resource increased to 95Mt at 1.15% Li2O.
  • Zijin Mining reports robust H1 2025 results: Net profit surges 54% to RMB 23.3 Billion.
  • POSCO's Lithium South Due Diligence substantially concluded, commencement of Definitive Agreement.

As usual all comments are welcome.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: Dirt Cheap, Keep Buying
data.description:
Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Super Micro Computer (NASDAQ:SMCI) (NEOE:SMCI:CA), I was particularly bullish. My call didn’t exactly play out as expected, but nor was it a total flop either. The stock has climbed almost 7% since then, and that’s a pretty respectable gain, even if slightly lower compared to the broader market.

The reason I liked SMCI before hasn’t changed: the company is thriving right in the heart of the AI boom and continues to keep itself ahead of the game. The long-term tailwinds for this company are strong, and despite the stock’s run so far this year, the valuation remains surprisingly cheap. All in all, I reiterate my "Strong Buy" rating.

Recent Developments

As we [SMCI bulls] all know very well, the company excels in servers for AI data centers. The drawback historically was that the company has been kind of "under-the-radar" compared to the most widely known AI names. However, the management is working in this direction, as the company recently participated in some quite notable industry events [one of them was held by SMCI].

The company recently presented at the prestigious Citi 2025 Global Technology, Media and Telecommunications Conference, and while events like this may sound a bit routine, they give the management direct access to heavyweight institutional investors. And a good presentation always helps attract fresh capital and boost confidence in the company itself. I have a few crucial takeaways from the event that reinforce SMCI's strategic positioning.

First, the management highlighted significant customer expansion, citing growth from 1 to 4 scale customers YoY, which shows the company's ability to penetrate enterprise-level accounts.

Second, the company also emphasized their strategic Data Center Building Block Solutions [DCBBS] approach as a key differentiator, which enables faster deployment and customization compared to traditional rack-scale solutions.

Another notable event was SMCI's recent "INNOVATE! EMEA 2025" event, which took place on September 22-23 in Madrid, Spain. First, it presents a good opportunity to increase awareness about SMCI in Europe, where data center infrastructure is developing rapidly and the total addressable market is expected to surpass $150 billion by 2030. Additionally, it served as an excellent platform to introduce some of the new edge systems and data center solutions, particularly showcasing cutting-edge AI servers with the latest NVIDIA GPUs [HGX B300, GB300 NVL72]. The important part is that the new advanced liquid cooling technology boasts reduced power consumption by up to 40%, which is vital for very energy-conscious Europe. Therefore, I think that the event went well to expand SMCI's European exposure.

Expanding geographic footprint is just one part of SMCI's growth strategy. The core part is, of course, aggressive innovation. I see it from the company's soaring R&D budget, which is vital to succeed because the niche is quite competitive with large players like Dell Technologies and Hewlett Packard Enterprise. Therefore, an aggressive pace of innovation is vital, and SMCI does quite well in this respect.

SMCI has begun volume shipments of NVIDIA’s next-generation Blackwell Ultra Systems ahead of competitors. Why is this important? Well, having a first-mover advantage among just a few companies to deliver these systems puts SMCI at the very heart of the next GPU upgrade cycle. Think about it: every data center that wants top-tier AI computing power will need hardware just like this, and SMCI is one of the key suppliers. Apart from being fast in delivering upgrades in line with Nvidia's new releases, SMCI also benefits from the strong personal relationship between their CEO, Charles Liang, and Jensen Huang.

Strategic partnerships are also important for the company's growth strategy. SMCI also recently announced it is partnering with Lambda, a leading company in AI infrastructure. Lambda is building what it terms “AI factories” using SMCI’s NVIDIA Blackwell GPU server clusters. Not only do these large-scale deployments showcase the company’s technology, but they also highlight its ability to deliver energy-efficient solutions—and this is what appeals to enterprise cloud customers who want to make cost savings.

All these strategic developments make me very bullish because they are happening in the midst of explosive industry growth. A comprehensive report from Global Market Insights estimates that the global AI server market alone was worth a staggering $128 billion in 2024 and will grow to an even bigger $167.2 billion by the end of 2025 and to $1.56 trillion by 2034, representing a 28.2% CAGR. These are the sorts of numbers to make anyone’s heart race. It is also this kind of ongoing demand that is without a doubt a dream scenario for a company like SMCI, specializing as it does in exactly the type of AI servers and data center hardware that are so highly sought after in the market right now.

The reason my previous bullish call didn’t age quite as planned is because SMCI reported a dual miss in FQ4 2025, meaning that both revenue and earnings fell below Wall Street expectations. While that sounds bad, let’s look at the context. The revenue ‘miss’ was only about $156 million compared to analysts’ forecasts and to the company’s $5.7 billion in quarterly sales. In other words, that shortfall was actually quite small. Then there’s the fact that the EPS rose sequentially, climbing from $0.31 to $0.41 compared to last quarter. None of these numbers scream trouble to me.

Moreover, YoY revenue growth remained solid in Q4 with an 8.5% increase. This is a notable deceleration compared to previous quarters, but it looks absolutely natural as comparative figures are significantly larger. Moreover, we can see [below] that FQ4 2024 was the exact quarter when SMCI's quarterly sales spiked from below $4 billion straight to above $5 billion. Therefore, it is no surprise why FQ4 2025 revenue growth was much slower compared to FQ3 2025 revenue growth. Moreover, Wall Street analysts expect quarterly revenue to start accelerating again very soon, which makes sense due to the aggressive growth strategy and industry tailwinds.

Some critics might say the company’s profitability isn’t growing, and yes, if we look purely at the margins, they are slightly worrying. But let’s remind ourselves that this is a company that operates in a highly competitive industry where price pressure is a normal part of the working day. In fact, in order to keep revenue growth strong, you often have to accept slightly lower margins in the short term. It’s also worth remembering that SMCI is part of the global semiconductor supply chain, which is prone to bottlenecks. This means the slightest hiccup in logistics can send costs rising and margins shrinking, albeit temporarily.

The company is also bolstered enough financially to handle any potential obstacles thrown in its way: it has an ample $5 billion cash cushion versus a low debt—$5 billion with a market cap of around $27 billion. Such a rock-solid balance sheet not only allows the management to pursue the aforementioned aggressive investment strategy in R&D, but it also gives it plenty of flexibility to explore opportunities in acquisitions or even engage in price wars against competitors if the need arises—and all without putting the company at financial risk.

To conclude the core part of my analysis, I believe there are too many positives that should not be ignored, especially considering SMCI's compelling valuation.

Valuation Update

Even after a good, solid year, SMCI’s stock is still fairly cheap. Over the last 12 months, the stock gained around 5% and is up 50% so far in 2025, considerably beating the S&P 500. This is normally the sort of shift that would push the stock into more expensive territory—but not here.

Despite the company’s growing revenue in an industry that’s clearly booming, SMCI’s forward Price/Sales ratio is still below 1. That’s extremely low. Typically you’d only see a P/S ratio this low for companies in industries that are either declining [like tobacco] or that are drowning in debt or unprofitable. Neither of these scenarios is the case here, especially as SMCI’s EPS is well above zero.

And in fact, Wall Street analysts are expecting this already strong EPS to grow further. Based on these forecasts, the FY2026 forward P/E ratio is well below 20, and for a company that’s ready to exploit the AI market, this is a valuation that seems shockingly cheap to me.

Risks Update

No investment is risk-free, and here are some that I believe investors should bear in mind when considering SMCI.

Last year, the company was heavily scrutinized over its financial reporting controls. While the management seems to have addressed these issues and appears keen to avoid a repeat of this happening, the memory of such scrutiny is likely to make some investors nervous.

Another risk is seasonality: if we look at the historical trading patterns, October tends to be a weaker month with a relatively low “win rate.” By contrast, November seems to be much stronger with a 100% win rate. While this doesn’t guarantee future results, it does suggest that investors should consider dollar-cost averaging, as in buying in stages in late September and October to avoid the effects of short-term volatility.

Competition is a factor, as SMCI operates in a highly competitive market with a constant stream of new technology being added. In short, the company will have to stay innovative to stay strong and ahead of the game. Equally risky are the company’s bottleneck-prone supply chains, which are easily disrupted. Any delay in obtaining critical components due to geopolitical events or natural disasters will instantly impact the company’s sales.

Bottom Line

With SMCI designing and building the very servers, storage systems, and data center hardware that drive everything from chatbots to self-driving cars, there’s no doubt the company represents a clear-cut opportunity to invest in the AI boom. While the most recent quarter wasn’t great with the dual miss, sales continue to grow, earnings are improving, and the management is out there raising the company’s profile at key investor events. The balance sheet is strong with plenty of cash to fund investment and acquisitions, and let’s not forget that breathtaking tenfold growth figure for the AI server market over the next decade. To conclude, SMCI remains a "Strong Buy."

data.title: Rhythm Pharmaceuticals: Exciting Times With sNDA PDUFA Date
data.description:
This is my third Rhythm (NASDAQ:RYTM) article, following 06/2025's "Rhythm Pharmaceuticals: Rare Genetic Obesity Pioneer With Excellent Prospects." In Excellent Prospects, I rated Rhythm as a "Buy." It has since enjoyed a tasty ~52% bump. In this article, I will assess its forward prospects to see if it is still an attractive investment candidate.

Rhythm is Motoring Ahead With Possible New Blockbuster Approval for IMCIVREE in 12/2025

IMCIVREE Existing Approvals

As reported in Excellent Prospects in 2020, IMCIVREE (setmelanotide) was approved to treat a variety of rare genetic obesity diseases. In 2022 it was approved to treat a more widely prevalent genetic obesity abnormality, Bardet-Biedl syndrome [BBS].

The panel below from Rhythm's 09/2025 website Presentation shows its estimates of the prevalence of IMCIVREE's currently approved indications:

Anticipated sNDA

In its 08/05/2025 Q2/2025 Financial Results and Business Update release, Rhythm listed the following near-term expected milestone:

Complete submissions of a supplemental New Drug Application to the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and a Type II variation request to the European Medicines Agency [EMA] seeking approval for setmelanotide for the treatment of acquired hypothalamic obesity [AHO] in the third quarter of 2025;

Just a short ~2 weeks later, on 08/21/2025, Rhythm issued a release announcing:

  1. the FDA has accepted Rhythm’s supplemental New Drug Application [sNDA] for setmelanotide in the treatment of AHO;
  2. the FDA has granted priority review for the sNDA, assigning 12/20/2025 as the PDUFA date;
  3. the EMA took analogous action, with its review expected to begin on 08/16/2025.

AHO Potential

If Rhythm is able to snag required approvals for IMCIVREE to treat AHO, it will significantly grow its potential market reach, as shown by its Presentation panel below:

An FDA approval for AHO in 12/2025 would expand IMCIVREE's reach by a factor of 2X or 3X. It is this potential that undergirds Goldman Sachs' IMCIVREE peak revenue estimate of $1.9 billion and Jefferies' of up to $3 billion.

In response to an analyst question during the Call as to the anticipated launch characteristics for AHO and the markets it would approach, CEO Meeker provided a thoughtful response, noting:

... AHO, [has a] bigger epidemiology [than BBS]. I mean we've had questions this morning, and we'll get -- continue to get a lot of questions about how big could this be. I think where we are now, a lot of confidence in our current projections -- could it be bigger? Absolutely.

Will it have some of the same dynamics as these kind of ultra-rare diseases? And AHO sits a little bit in the middle. It's absolutely a rare disease, quite rare, 5,000 to 10,000 put it in the very rare category, but it's very specialty like given the concentration, more patients diagnosed, an attentive specialty by definition. So AHO may have a slightly different ramp, if you will, steeper than BBS because of those factors. But the other aspects of AHO are going to be very similar, which is think about steady growth over time.

Don't think about inflecting this is something and explode out of the gates and way some of these "specialty opportunities do." That's going to be more rare like, but it will be steeper and it will grow for a very long time. And we may be wrong in the epidemiology, meaning that it could be larger and given enough time, I think that's likely. ...

This anticipated long development path walks us right up to the next critical question—what kind of IP protection does IMCIVREE have? Unfortunately, the answer is not long enough.

Rhythm's Patent Extension Strategy is Riddled With Risks

Rhythm's website Presentation slide 10 below shows how Rhythm is making an all-out attack on AHO, not just with IMCIVREE (setmelanotide), but also with Bivamelagon, and RM-718:

This is a rare concentration of focus on a single indication. What gives?

Obviously, there are lots of rationale for attacking a particular disease with multiple molecules. It is always good to have a backup. Different molecules involve different potential interactions and have different modes of delivery:

  1. IMCIVREE—injected subcutaneously once daily;
  2. Bivamelagon—oral
  3. RM-718—injection with work on auto-injector in progress.

The issue, which I suspect is the biggest driver of focus on these two next-generation therapies, is their extended patent life. During the Call Meeker advised:

...Setmelanotide composition of matter patent is up in 2032, but importantly, the formulation patents extend to 2034 in the U.S. Our next-generation compounds will extend that protection to 2040 plus.

Given the extended path expected for full realization of AHO revenues, setmelanotide's modest remaining patent term is insufficient. In order for Bivamelagon and RM-718 to work as patent extenders, they will need to both:

  1. secure FDA approval to treat AHO with comparable or more favorable labels; and
  2. succeed in the marketplace against likely lower-priced IMCIVREE generics.

Rhythm's Liquidity Supports a Modest Cash Runway.

During the Call CFO Smith advised that Rhythm had cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments of $291 million as of 06/30/2025. He also advised that in 07/2025 Rhythm paid $40 million to LG Chem as part of its Bivamelagon acquisition obligation.

He went on to advise that:

...Rhythm's cash on hand, combined with the net proceeds from last month's stock offering, forecasted revenues from the anticipated launch of IMCIVREE acquired HO as well as ongoing revenue from approved indications and currently planned R&D and commercial activity provides cash runway of at least 24 months.

Rhythm does not issue any revenue guidance. A two-year runway dating from 06/30/2025 is not much of a runway. Unless its liquidity takes an unexpected positive move upward, I expect it to be looking for additional financing at some point in the next few quarters.

Its Q2/2025 10-Q lists the following menu of potential fundraising options:

...proceeds from the issuance of debt, sale of equity, proceeds from out license arrangements, product sales and funded research and development programs...

Conclusion

While I remain a staunch Rhythm bull, I do expect it to rest and to consolidate its gains through the balance of 2025 into Q1/2025. I am dropping my "Buy" rating to "Hold".

I consider the biggest risks on the plus side to be:

  1. an unexpected acceleration in revenues;
  2. an out-license deal that provides a significant liquidity boost without dilution.

I consider the biggest risk on the negative side would be for the FDA to issue a CRL.

data.title: RFIX: Long Rates With Leverage
data.description:
Thesis

The long end of the treasury curve is an interesting place, but also a high-duration place. High duration translates into volatility when it comes to bonds, and substantial gains or losses can be had. Suffice to look at a historic graph of the widely utilized iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT):

In the past decade, TLT has seen substantial 'action,' with gains exceeding 25% during Covid when rates plummeted, and a massive -50% drawdown since, as long rates moved above 4%.

Now while some readers might be scared by such volatility, one might be surprised to find out there are funds that offer 2x or even 3x the return profile of TLT.

In today's article we are going to discuss an interesting fund from Simplify Asset Management that takes a leveraged position on the long end, namely the Simplify Bond Bull ETF (NYSEARCA:RFIX).

What does the fund do? A leveraged take on the long end.

Simplify always structures its funds in an interesting way, and RFIX is no different. The ETF uses swaptions (options on swaps) in order to create a synthetic leveraged position on the long end of the curve. We have covered the RFIX mirror fund before, namely the Simplify Interest Rate Hedge ETF (PFIX) which we discussed here. Both names use swaptions to create their desired payoff profiles. Traditional ETFs usually use total return swaps or swaps with large banks to get a defined payoff profile.

RFIX is designed to profit when long rates fall and volatility rises, and is a fairly new fund having IPO-ed in 2024. Some of the fund analytics are:

  • AUM: $0.12 bil
  • Expense ratio: 0.5%
  • Duration: 41 years

Its current holdings composition is as follows:

We can observe the name parking its cash in T-Bills, while having a number of swaptions and swaps with Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs.

The most interesting aspect is the result of this composition, and we shall explore that aspect in the next section.

Return profile

Via its composition, RFIX represents a leveraged take on the long end of the curve, and competes with several funds already in that space:

We are comparing RFIX here with the Direxion Daily 20+ Year Treasury Bull 3x Shares (TMF) and the ProShares Ultra 20+ Year Treasury (UBT) funds. While TMF states in its name the goals, UBT has the following as its objective - "seeks to replicate twice the daily performance of the ICE U.S. Treasury 20+ Year Bond Index, by using a full replication technique." So TMF is a 3x daily fund, while UBT is a 2x daily fund.

As we can see from the graph above, all three names had a similar performance up to June/July 2025, when TMF and UBT started to gain value, while RFIX plummeted. The reason? Volatility:

Remember that RFIX is long swaptions, thus has a vega and a delta component. While long rates did move lower, volatility also plummeted by almost 25% (from roughly 100 to 75 currently) as measured by the widely used bond volatility index 'MOVE':

While the VIX can help you prepare for stock market shifts—especially downward ones—the Merrill Lynch Option Volatility Estimate, commonly known as the MOVE Index (MOVE:GIF), tracks volatility in Treasuries to provide insight into the fixed income market and sometimes can even outpace the VIX as a red flag. Fixed income is the second largest market in the world behind currencies.

So RFIX needs both lower rates and higher or constant volatility in order to thrive. Since June 2025, it has underperformed its cohort due to much lower levels in volatility and the MOVE index.

At the end of the day, an investor putting money in RFIX is betting long rates are heading lower, and volatility is moving higher from current levels. We are going to show you in the next section why we believe the long end of the curve will stay elevated despite Fed Funds moving lower.

Fed funds are moving lower, but the long end is a different story

While many people are discussing the recent Fed cuts, investors need to realize that cutting Fed Funds does not mean that long dated yields will also come down. The Fed controls the front end of the yield curve, but the markets control longer durations:

In general, two-year Treasury yields can be seen as a compounding of two years' worth of daily federal funds rate expectations, with small adjustments for liquidity disparities and other factors. However, when it comes to 10-year Treasuries, factors like inflation, economic growth and Federal Reserve’s (Fed) monetary policy, among others, play important roles.

In fact, the market reaction to the recent Fed cut was the opposite of what some might have thought, with long yields moving higher:

Traders of longer-dated bonds “don’t want the Fed to be cutting interest rates,” Boockvar said. Their selling of long-term bonds drove down the price and drove up the yield. Prices and yields for bonds move in an inverse direction. Easing monetary policy at a time when inflation is running above the Fed’s 2% target and the economy looks steady can indicate the central bank is “taking the eye off” inflation, Boockvar said, a key risk to longer duration securities.

Unlike pandemic events like COVID, when the Fed engaged in quantitative easing to ease conditions in the economy, a slow and gradual move to a neutral Fed Funds rate might see just the front end of the yield curve move. And then we have the potential return of inflation, with the July 2025 PPI figures hitting a 3-year high:

While a resurgence of inflationary pressures is not guaranteed, the figures coming out of the economy coupled with tariff pressures are indeed very worrisome. Several research houses see the same scenario playing out:

With inflation elevated and fiscal deficits rising, there may not be much room for Treasury yields to fall much further, especially those with longer-term maturities. We look for a modest move down in short-to-intermediate term Treasury yields over the next few months as the Federal Reserve lowers the federal funds rate, but long-term yields are likely to remain elevated in the absence of falling inflation and/or a decline in fiscal deficits.

At the end of the day, it is about fiscal deficits and inflation. If these two are brought under control, there is scope for lower long rates. As of the writing of this article, there is no indication that either of the two are now fixed.

Conclusion

RFIX is an exchange-traded fund. The name represents a leveraged take on long rates, and makes money when 20-year rates move lower while volatility is also high. The ETF represents a similar payoff profile as TMF and UBT, but has the added component of volatility. RFIX has lost value this year as long rates have remained high and volatility has come down. We are of the opinion that the current fiscal deficit and inflationary pressures will keep the long end of the yield curve elevated, thus are a 'Sell' for RFIX.

data.title: Amtech Systems: Upgrading To Buy On Bullish AI-Related Equipment Revenue Growth
data.description:
Intro

Our last coverage on Amtech Systems, Inc. (NASDAQ:ASYS) was in July 2023, when we stamped a 'Hold' rating on the semiconductor-oriented material & equipment supplier. Shares were trading at $9.25 per share at the time. The company's keen valuation, positive bottom-line profitability, and strong balance sheet were all bullish indicators, although we awaited a breakout from an intermediate symmetrical triangle at the time before potentially putting capital to work on the long side. Usually, symmetrical triangles or coils represent a continuation pattern (bullish), but as we see below, this did NOT come to pass in late 2023.

In fact, ASYS stock stooped to well below $4 a share in early 2024 before finally making a lower low in April of this year. Suffice it to say, we reiterate that micro-cap stock investors should always wait until defined trends are in motion before investing capital dollars. As noted above, the lack of trading volume many times can nullify share-price action and adjoining technical patterns, especially when buying or indeed selling in this case at an accelerated clip. Therefore, irrespective of the spectacular gains ASYS stock has made since those April lows earlier this year, shares are still down roughly 3% since our commentary roughly 27 months ago and are not coming up against significant overhead resistance, as we see below.

Long-term Chart: Better Read on Investor Psychology

Therefore, given the below-average trading volumes in these plays, it always makes sense to consult long-term charts, as short-term rallies (like the stock has experienced in recent months) can be emotionally driven, all things remaining equal. To get a better perspective of how ASYS investors view this stock, we see from the long-term chart below that shares have basically traded in a range-bound manner for over a decade. This gives further credence to what we alluded to earlier in that ASYS stock (given the strength of the current multi-month rally) could rally to something like $12 over the near term, but anything beyond this point remains an uncertainty at this stage (remember: investor psychology tends not to change over the long term).

Near-term Fundamentals Improving

The company's third quarter of the present fiscal year (Q3 earnings announced 8/9/2025) has been the most near-term catalyst for the present trending move. Amtech's Q3 revenues of $19.6 million came in well above expectations, growing by 26% over the first quarter. What was encouraging, however, from a fundamental standpoint, is that both the 'Thermal Processing Solutions' segment as well as 'Semiconductor Fabrication' revenues surprised to the upside, resulting in much improved investor sentiment and share-price gains to boot. AI infrastructure-related spending continues to pay dividends for Amtech in Thermal Processing. Furthermore, the expansion of recurring revenue is taking place alongside growing capital equipment sales, as we learned from the CEPO below.

Related to revenue mix, we generated about 60% of our revenue from capital equipment and 40% from recurring revenue, including consumables, parts, and services. The balance between capital equipment and recurring revenue is important and reflects our strategy to expand recurring revenue streams while fully capitalizing on opportunities for equipment used to expand AI infrastructure. As we look ahead, our third-quarter bookings suggest we should continue to see strength for AI-related equipment revenue.

In the Semiconductor Fabrication Solutions segment, while weak mature node demand persisted, Q3 results came in better than expected, especially in the consumables area. Suffice it to say, although Amtech has been becoming a smaller outfit, management believes that plenty of value can be created through robust AI-related demand, operational discipline, and focused investments in the likes of semiconductor packaging equipment and applications alike in the Semiconductor Fabrication Solutions segment.

Favourable Gross Margin Trends

Amtech's gross profit of $9.1 million in Q3 benefited from an employee-related refund to the tune of $1 million. However, even if we exclude the employee retention credit, gross margin for the quarter topped 41%, which was an excellent readout compared to the same quarter of 12 months previous (36.5%). The above trend enabled the company to report a Q3 operating profit of $1.4 million (compared to $0.9 million in Q3 of fiscal 2024), despite the fact that revenues in Q3 came in $7.1 million shy of their year-over-year comparable.

Suffice it to say, when a company can downsize the number of manufacturing sites it runs and yet keep on growing its margins and profitability, it speaks volumes for the stock concerned. Furthermore, a rising gross margin metric can act as an excellent hedge against rising inflation over time. Since margin gains usually maintain themselves down the income statement, it boils down to how much more gains can be extracted over time. Management did point to the potential for further savings from subletting unutilized facilities and the fact that recent cost reduction initiatives have yet to hit the income statement (further operational-led margin gains expected).

Revenues May Remain Lumpy, but Revisions Remain Encouraging

Therefore, given how strong AI equipment bookings are expected to offset muted node product demand to some degree, we will be keeping an eye on how margins fare out, as the timing of orders/shipments may affect top-line results quarterly. However, on an annual basis, forward-looking revisions look favourable with upward revisions for both revenues and earnings outnumbering downward revisions.

Conclusion

Therefore, given Amtech's strong multi-month rally, growing margins, and better-than-expected top-line results, we are upgrading the stock to a 'Buy' until further notice. Amtech stock looks like it has further to run here, where a move to the $12 level would return over 23% for qualifying shareholders. We look forward to continued coverage.
